if x is a solution and

We also assume that there is a black box (an “oracle”)
U _ {f} | x \rangle | q \rangle = | x \rangle | q \oplus f (x) \rangle \tag {24.1}
, and the oracle that tests x is one of the four gates:
Fig. 24.4 Oracles for the four cases: solution

The first two qubits (the “query” qubits) encode
the last qubit is the oracle
response. The circuit for the search of the solution is:

```mermaid
graph LR
  H1["H"] --> Oracle["Oracle"]
  H2["H"] --> Oracle
  H3["H"] --> Oracle
  Oracle --> H1
  Oracle --> H2
  Oracle --> H3
  H1 --> X["X"]
  H2 --> X
  H3 --> X
  X --> H1

  H1 --> X
  H2 --> H1

  H2 --> X

  X --> H2
  H1 --> X
  H2 --> X
  X --> H1
  H1 --> H2
  H2 --> H1
  X --> H2
  H1 --> H1
  H2 --> H2
  H1 --> H1
  H2 --> H2
```
Fig. 24.5 circuit for the search of the solution
The problem is to find, in a set of
elements, a subset of M elements
that satisfies some given conditions. We say then that the search problem has M
We can assume the existence of a function
from n bits to 1 bit, that takes
if x is a solution and
if x is not a solution.
We also assume that there is a black box (an “oracle”)
U _ {f} | x \rangle | q \rangle = | x \rangle | q \oplus f (x) \rangle \tag {24.1}
, and the oracle that tests x is one of the four gates:
Fig. 24.4 Oracles for the four cases: solution

The first two qubits (the “query” qubits) encode
the last qubit is the oracle

Fig. 24.5 circuit for the search of the solution
Initially, the two query qubits are in the state <sub>|</sub>00<sub>i</sub>, and the last qubit in the state
<sub>|</sub>1<sub>i</sub>. Only one iteration is required to obtain exactly
circuit, that the measurement of the top two qubits gives
By contrast, a classical computer trying to find
by examining all the two-bit x
(four of them) would require an average of 2.25 oracle calls.
